coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensing performance of nitrogen rich zero- and two-dimensional carbon nanomaterials modified electrodes towards purines catabolism
coredata.dc:creator: Ajith, Ajay
John, S. Abraham
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical sensing of uric acid (UA) by using nitrogen-rich carbon nanomaterials such as N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) electrodeposited on screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was studied. These N-rich carbon materials were prepared by reported procedures and characterized by spectroscopic, XRD and SEM techniques. They were deposited on SPCE by potentiodynamic electrodeposition from -0.2 to +1.7 V. The topographical studies by SEM reveal that the dots like morphology of both N-CDs and N-GQDs was retained on SPC substrate whereas aggregated thin sheet like structure was noticed for the electrodeposited GCN. Then, the electrochemical sensing performance of these electrodes towards UA was studied. Among the different electrodes, SPCE deposited with GCN (SPCE/GCN) shows the highest oxidation current for UA due to its larger surface area and lower charge transfer resistance. Further, SPCE/GCN was exploited to determine UA in the presence of xanthine (XA), which was commonly present in blood serum. The amperometric method was deployed to determine UA from 0.2 to 1000 µM and achieved the limit of detection (LOD) of 24.4 nM (S/N=3) and sensitivity of 12.4 µA µM−1 cm−2. The practical application of SPCE/GCN was examined by quantifying UA in human blood serum sample and the obtained result was validated with the clinical method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
N-doped carbon dots
N-doped graphene quantum dots
Uric acid
Xanthine
Screen printed carbon electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs
Fabrication of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs on SPCE
Characterization of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs by different techniques
Modification of SPCE with GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs
Characterization of electrodeposited GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs on SPCE
UA oxidation at SPCE modified with carbon nanomaterials
Sensitive and selective determination of UA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial assistance from the Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi , ( IDP/MED/04/2017 ) is indebtedly acknowledgd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns obtained for GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs powders coated on glass plates.
Raman spectra obtained for GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs powders coated on glass plates.
SEM images obtained for bare SPC substrate and GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs electrodeposited SPC substrates.

Nyquist plots for bare and N-GQDs, N-CDs and GCN electrodeposited SPCEs in 1 mM K3/K4[Fe(CN)6] with 0.1 M KCl from 0.01 to 100,000 Hz. Inset: equivalent electrical circuit used for fitting the impedance spectra.

CVs obtained for the oxidation of 0.5 mM of UA at bare SPCE, N-GQDs, N-CDs, GCN electrodeposited SPCEs in 0.2 M PB solution (pH 7.2) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Solid line: second cycle; dotted line: sixth cycle.
SEM images obtained for SPC substrate modified with GCN before (a) and after (a’) voltammetric measurements in the presence of 0.5 mM UA containing 0.2 M PB solution.
DPVs obtained for (a) in absence of UA and XA and (b-k) each increment of 5 µM each UA and XA at SPCE/GCN in 0.2 M PB solution (pH 7.2).

Amperometric i-t curve obtained for each addition of (a) 0.2, (b) 0.5, (c) 1, (d) 2, (e) 5 (f) 10, (g) 20, (h) 50, (i) 100, (j) 200, (k) 500 and (l) 1000 µM UA at SPCE/GCN in 0.2 M PB solution (pH 7.2) at regular intervals of 50 s. Eapp= +0.4 V (Inset: (A) expanded view from

DPVs obtained for absence (a) and (b) presence of blood serum at SPCE/GCN. After the addition of (c) 20, (d) 40 and (e) 60 µM UA to the blood serum. (f) addition of 20 µM XA to (e) in 0.2 M PB solution pH (7.2).
Scheme showing the electrodeposited GCN, N-CDS and N-GQDs on SPCEs.
Determination of UA in blood serum sample using SPCE/GCN and comparison of the results with enzymatic method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Three replicate measurements were made on the sample.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensing performance of nitrogen rich zero- and two-dimensional carbon nanomaterials modified electrodes towards purines catabolism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, The Gandhigram Rural Institute-Deemed to be University, Gandhigram, Dindigul, Tamil Nadu 624 302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, The Gandhigram Rural Institute-Deemed to be University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, The Gandhigram Rural Institute-Deemed to be University, Gandhigram, Dindigul, Tamil Nadu 624 302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical sensing of uric acid (UA) by using nitrogen-rich carbon nanomaterials such as N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) electrodeposited on screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was studied. These N-rich carbon materials were prepared by reported procedures and characterized by spectroscopic, XRD and SEM techniques. They were deposited on SPCE by potentiodynamic electrodeposition from -0.2 to +1.7 V. The topographical studies by SEM reveal that the dots like morphology of both N-CDs and N-GQDs was retained on SPC substrate whereas aggregated thin sheet like structure was noticed for the electrodeposited GCN. Then, the electrochemical sensing performance of these electrodes towards UA was studied. Among the different electrodes, SPCE deposited with GCN (SPCE/GCN) shows the highest oxidation current for UA due to its larger surface area and lower charge transfer resistance. Further, SPCE/GCN was exploited to determine UA in the presence of xanthine (XA), which was commonly present in blood serum. The amperometric method was deployed to determine UA from 0.2 to 1000 µM and achieved the limit of detection (LOD) of 24.4 nM (S/N=3) and sensitivity of 12.4 µA µM−1 cm−2. The practical application of SPCE/GCN was examined by quantifying UA in human blood serum sample and the obtained result was validated with the clinical method.

Nitrogen rich carbon nanomaterials, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) were electrodeposited on screen printed carbon electrode and compared their performance towards electrochemical sensing of uric acid.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, considerable attention is directed to utilize low cost nitrogen rich carbon nanomaterials for photocatalytic and electrocatalytic applications, which include graphitic carbon nitride (GCN), nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) besides nitrogen doped reduced graphene oxide [1–10]. Among these materials, both N-CDs and N-GQDs fall under zero dimensional materials whereas GCN is categorized as 2-D layered materials, comprising a π-conjugated graphitic plane containing sp2 hybridized carbon and nitrogen atoms [11,12]. Although the photocatalytic applications of these three materials have been extensively reported in the literature, only limited papers are available for their electrocatalytic applications. Pumera and his co-workers concluded that GCN possesses poor electrical conductivity and hence it is not suitable for electrochemical sensing applications [13]. In contrary, our research group very recently found that the protocol used for the preparation of GCN sheet is very important to extract its electrocatalytic activity [14]. For example, the glassy carbon (GC) electrode modified with GCN after exfoliated in water for 30 min showed lower electrocatalytic activity than bare GC electrode. However, 120 min exfoliated GCN showed higher oxidation current than GC electrode [14]. Unlike GCN, the electrochemical sensing applications of N-CDs and N-GQDs are very limited [4,5,15–18]. In the case of CDs, the possible π-π and hydrogen bonding interactions between the analyte and CDs are the two major reasons for their high electrocatalytic activity towards the analytes. Two reports are available in the literature for the electrochemical sensing application of GQDs modified electrode [17,18]. Both the papers reported the electrochemical sensing of dopamine using self-assembled GQDs and GQDs composite with Nafion. Based on the above studies, it is very difficult to conclude which N-rich material is suitable for electrochemical sensing applications. Our research group studied the electrochemical sensing application of N-CDs and GCN modified electrodes by taking different analytes including uric acid (UA), tyrosine, nitrobenzene, ascorbic acid and hydrogen peroxide. But, not exploited the electrochemical sensing application of N-GQDs modified electrode. Thus, the current study aims to find an appropriate N-rich carbon material among GCN, N-GQDs and N-CDs for the electrochemical sensing of UA. The reasons for choosing UA sensing are discussed below.

Purine catabolism in human beings and higher primates leads to the production of UA. It is well known that any alteration in serum UA concentration leads to several complications [19–25]. For example, UA elevation into abnormally high levels is associated with gout [19], hypertension [20,21], cardiovascular [22,23] and renal diseases [24,25]. Gout is an inflammatory reaction primarily which occurs in the joints and forms UA crystals [19]. As the concentration of UA increases, UA crystal formation increases and this could enhance the vulnerability of gout. The cause for the elevation of UA in serum includes the consumption of alcohol and a diet that contains high in purines and proteins [26]. Therefore, monitoring UA concentration in biological fluids is very important since it can be used as an early warning for the aforementioned diseases. Besides UA, xanthine (XA) is another important purine catabolite and abnormal level of XA can lead to xanthinuria, which indirectly converted to UA by the action of xanthine oxidase [27]. Therefore, sensing of UA in the presence of XA is very important. Thus, the objective of the current study is to electrodeposit N-GQDs, N-CDs and GCN with the aid of the free amine functional groups available on the respective carbon nanomaterials on screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) and study the performance of the resulting electrodes towards the oxidation of UA and XA. The voltammetric results reveal that GCN deposited on SPCE showed higher electrocatalytic activity than N-GQDs and N-CDs towards the oxidation of UA. Thus, it was selected for the sensitive and selective determination of UA.

The reagents used in the current study were of analytical grade and the details are specified in the supporting information (SI), instruments used for the current investigation were furnished in the SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g melamine was heated in air at 600 °C for 120 min at 25 °C per minute. The colorless powder was turned into a pale yellow color, indicating GCN formation [28]. By melting L-asparagine at 100 °C yields N-CDs and colorless powder turns to brown color within 20 min and then it was diluted with 0.1 M NaOH [29]. N-GQDs were prepared by dissolving 1 mM citric acid and 3 mM urea in 50 mL water and transferred to a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated at 160 °C in a hot air oven for 8 h. The final product was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove the unreacted compounds [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs on SPCE
ce:section.ce:para: SPCEs were modified with GCN by potentiodynamic electrodeposition between −0.2 to +1.7 V (15 cycles, 0.05 V s−1) in 1 mg mL−1 of GCN containing 0.01 M H2SO4. Similar procedure was repeated to prepare N-GQDs and N-CDs with 50 mg mL−1 for 10 continuous potential cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs by different techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 depicts the UV-vis spectra of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs colloidal solutions. GCN shows the absorption band at 320 nm due to π-π* transition. However, N-CDs and N-GQDs show a shoulder band at 307 nm and a sharp peak at 330 nm, respectively due to n-π* transition. The FT-IR spectra of as-prepared N-CDs, N-GQDs and GCN are shown in Fig. S2. The FT-IR bands and corresponding vibrational frequencies were provided in Table S1. The GCN shows a major peak at 808 cm−1, corresponding to a breathing mode of s-triazine moiety [31]. N-CDs show a NH deformation peak at 1398 cm−1, NH and OH stretching vibrations emerged as a broad wave at 3450 cm−1 [29]. In the case of N-GQDs, CN bending vibration peak was noticed at 1401 cm−1 whereas a broad wave at 3400 cm−1 was appeared for NH and OH stretching [30]. Fig. 1 depicts the XRD pattern of powder GCN, N-CDS and N-GQDs coated on glass plates. GCN shows an intense dominant peak at 27.9° (002) corresponding to the interlayer spacing of CN layers and a weak diffraction pattern at 13.1° (100), ascribed to the in-plane tri-s-triazine motif. N-CDs and N-GQDs show diffraction peaks at 27.1° (002) and 29.7° (002) corresponding to individual interlayer spacing as depicted in Fig. 1, respectively.

Fig. 2 exhibits the Raman spectra of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs. GCN reveals a broad wave at 1355 cm−1, corresponding to the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon and nitrogen atoms [13]. The D band was appeared at 1365 cm−1 whereas G band noticed at 1568 cm−1 for N-CDs [32]. These bands were due to sp3 and sp2 hybridized atoms, respectively. N-GQDs show characteristic bands at 1355 and 1579 cm−1 due to the disordered D band arising as a result of sp3 defects and crystalline G band, respectively [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of SPCE with GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S3 shows the continuous CVs (15 cycles) recorded for GCN containing 0.01 M H2SO4 from −0.20 to +1.7 V on SPCE at 0.05 V s−1. In each cycle, the faradaic charge increases, revealing the deposition of conducting GCN on the SPCE. Fig. S3B and C exhibit the continuous CVs (10 cycles) obtained for N-CDs and N-GQDs containing 0.01 M H2SO4. In the case of N-CDs, an oxidation wave at +1.21 V was obtained in the first cycle and in the next cycle onwards, it was decreased and then disappeared after fifteen cycles. Further deposition of conducting N-CDs film on SPCE during each cycle was confirmed by observing an increment in faradaic current. The N-GQDs show an anodic peak at 0.84 V along with a sharp peak at 1.54 V in the first cycle and next cycle onwards the peak at 0.84 V was disappeared whereas the peak at 1.54 V was decreased initially and started increasing gradually, indicating the formation of conducting N-GQDs film on SPCE.

The mechanism for the electrodeposition of GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs is likely to be identical. It has been reported that during anodic deposition of compounds containing amine groups, a CN linkage was initially formed between amine group and carbon from glassy carbon (GC) electrode [33]. Similar CN bond is also expected to form during the electrodeposition of all the three carbon nanomaterials because all of them containing amine groups and carbon from SPCE. The deposited GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs on SPCE were schematically shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of electrodeposited GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs on SPCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S4 depicts the ATR-FT-IR spectra of electrodeposited GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs films on SPCE and their corresponding peak assignments are given in Table S1. In contrast to GCN powder, the characteristic s-triazine breathing mode of GCN was slightly shifted to 806 cm−1 after electrodeposited on the SPC substrate. The other vibrations due to NH stretching and CN were noticed with a slight shift. In the case of electrodeposited N-CDs, the broad band due to NH and OH stretching vibrations were shifted to 3200 cm−1 and all other characteristic peaks were slightly shifted. The N-GQDs film shows a broad band around 3200 cm−1 due to NH and OH stretching vibrations.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of bare and GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs electrodeposited SPC substrates. The bare SPC substrate displays a flake-like structure with few unevenly sized particles distributed over the surface. When the SPC substrate was modified by electrodeposition of GCN, aggregated broken sheet-like structures were noticed throughout the surface. The SEM image of electrodeposited N-CDs shows dot-like morphology with a size of ∼30 nm. Similarly, electrodeposited N-GQDs on SPC substrate showed a dot-like structure with a size of ∼20 nm. The SEM images of both N-CDs and N-GQDs indicate that the distribution of dots was not uniform and available only on smooth surfaces of SPC substrate.

The electroactive surface area (ESA) of electrodeposited GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs on SPCEs was determined by the chronocoloumetric method. 1 mM K4/K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KCl was used as the redox probe. The ESA can be calculated by Eq. (1) [34].
(1)
A =
a /
2 nFC
D
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
where, A = electroactive surface area (cm2), a = slope (C/s1/2), n = number of electrons transferred in redox reaction, D = diffusion coefficient of the redox probe (6.2 × 10−6 cm2/s), C = concentration of the redox probe (mol/cm3) and F = Faraday's constant (C/mol). The ESA of the bare SPCE and SPCE modified with N-GQDs, N-CDs and GCN was found to be 0.043, 0.084, 0.109 and 0.166 cm2, respectively, indicating that all the modified electrodes showed higher electroactive surface area than bare SPCE. For instance, electrodeposited N-GQDs, N-CDs and GCN showed 2-, 2.5- and 4-fold higher ESA, respectively in contrast to bare SPCE. Among the three modified electrodes, GCN showed the highest ESA. It is evident from the SEM image that the broken sheets of GCN were uniformly covered the entire SPC substrate (Fig. 3). This is the cause for the obtained higher ESA for GCN than N-CDs and N-GQDs electrodeposited on SPC substrates. However, the dot-like structures of N-CDs and N-GQDs were not covered on the entire surface of SPC as evidenced from Fig. 3. This may be the cause for the lower ESA of N-CDs and N-GQDs when compared to GCN.

Fig. 4 illustrates the Nyquist plots for bare and carbon nanomaterials deposited SPC electrodes in a mixture of 1 mM of K3/K4[Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 M KCl from 0.01 to 100,000 Hz. The impedance data were fitted by using Randles circuit model (inset: Fig. 4). Randles circuit model is built with the components like Rs indicating the electrolyte resistance, Cdl is the double layer capacitance, RCT is nothing but the charge transfer resistance, Zw the Warburg impedance. The RCT values for bare SPC, SPCEs modified with N-GQDs, N-CDs and GCN were estimated to be 24.8, 7.70, 4.13 and 2.96 kΩ, respectively. Among the three modified electrodes, N-GQDs showed the highest RCT value whereas GCN showed the least. These results indirectly reveal that the electrodeposited GCN is more conductive than other two modified electrodes.

The electron transfer rate constant (k et) was estimated from Eq. (2) [35].
(2)
k
e t
= RT /
n
2
F
2
A
R CT
C o
where R, T, F, n, Co and A have their usual meaning. k et for bare and SPCEs modified with N-GQDs, N-CDs and GCN was found to be 1.53, 4.93, 9.19 and 12.81 × 10−4 cm2 s−1, respectively. The obtained higher k et values for the modified electrodes reveal that electron transfer was more facile at the modified electrodes when compared to bare SPCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UA oxidation at SPCE modified with carbon nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 demonstrates the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of 0.5 mM UA at SPCEs modified with N-GQDS, N-CDs and GCN. The bare SPCE shows an oxidation peak for UA at 0.47 V with a current of 8.2 µA. After five cycles, the oxidation current of UA was decreased besides its potential was shifted to more positive potential (dotted curve). This is ascribed to the adsorption of allantoin (oxidized product of UA) on the SPCE. Thus, bare SPCE was not suitable for the stable determination of UA. The SPCE/N-GQDs oxidizes UA at 0.27 V with a current of 12 µA whereas UA oxidation peak appears at 0.22 V with a current of 13 µA at SPCE/N-CDs. The oxidation of UA occurs at 0.21 V with a current of 17.7 µA at SPCE/GCN. The dotted curves shown in Fig. 5 correspond to the electrode response after the fifth cycle. As illustrated in Fig. 5, the electrochemical response of UA was unaltered at all the modified electrodes after five cycles, indicating the absence of fouling effect. The UA oxidation current at SPCE/GCN was 2.2-, 1.3-, and 1.4-fold higher than bare and N-CDs and N-GQDs electrodeposited SPCEs, respectively. Besides, the oxidation potential of UA at SPCE/GCN was 260, 60 and 10 mV less positive potential than bare SPCE, SPCE/N-GQDs and SPCE/N-CDs, respectively.

Further, to find whether the oxidation of UA at the modified electrodes was adsorption or diffusion controlled, the CVs were recorded at different scan rates. It was found that while increasing the scan rate, oxidation current of UA was increased linearly (Fig. S5). The plot of current vs. square root of scan rate was linear with correlation coefficients of 0.9998, 0.9985 and 0.9981 for SPCEs modified with GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs, respectively (Fig. S6). Thus, the oxidation of UA at the modified electrodes was diffusion controlled. Further, the morphology of each modified surface was analyzed by SEM after voltammetric measurements. Fig. 6 illustrates the SEM image of SPCE modified with GCN before (a) and after (a’) voltammetric measurements. The aggregated sheet like morphology persists as such even after repeatedly carried out the oxidation of UA. This suggests that the sheet like morphology of GCN was not drastically affected. Fig. S7 depicts the SEM images of N-CDs and N-GQDs electrodeposited SPCEs substrates before (a and b) and after (a’ and b’), carried out the oxidation of UA. As evidenced from Fig. S7, the dot like morphology of N-CDs and N-GQDs remains unaltered, indicating that the surface morphology was unaffected after the electrochemical oxidation of UA.

The reason of the higher oxidation current of UA at these modified electrodes in contrast to bare SPCE is owing to the existence of π-π and H-bonding interactions between UA and N-rich carbon nanomaterials. The SPCE/GCN shows the highest electrocatalytic activity than SPCE/N-CDs and SPCE/N-GQDs because of its larger electroactive surface area and lower RCT. Thus, SPCE/GCN is chosen to for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective determination of UA
ce:section.ce:para: DPVs acquired for sequential addition of 5 µM UA at SPCE/GCN containing 0.2 M PB solution (pH 7.2) are depicted in Fig. S8. It was evident that upon each addition of 5 µM UA, the oxidation current was increased linearly (R2=0.9968) and the potential remains unchanged. This indicates that the adsorption of oxidized product does not occur at the SPCE/GCN.

Xanthine (XA) and UA are products after purine catabolism in human and higher primates [27]. Therefore, the electrochemical sensing of UA was investigated in the presence of XA using SPCE/GCN. Fig. 7 displays the DPVs obtained for the simultaneous determination of UA and XA. In the absence of UA and XA, the SPCE/GCN does not show any voltammetric response. Upon the addition of 5 µM each UA and XA, the oxidation of UA occurs at 0.18 V whereas oxidation of XA occurs at 0.57 V and the peak-peak potential difference of 390 mV was observed. On each addition of 5 µM UA and XA, the oxidation current of the respective compounds was increased linearly without affecting its potential.

Fig. S9A manifests the DPVs of XA containing 1 mM of UA at SPCE/GCN. On each increment of 10 µM XA (curve a-f), the oxidation current of XA increased linearly without affecting its potential of UA. Thus, SPCE/GCN could be used to determine XA containing 100-fold higher concentration of UA. Concurrently, SPCE/GCN can also be used to selectively determine UA in the presence of 0.5 mM of XA. Fig. S9B shows the DPVs acquired for each increment of 10 µM UA (curve a-f) containing 50-fold higher concentration of XA. As evidenced from Fig. S9B, the oxidation current of UA was increased while increasing its concentration without affecting the potential of XA. These results convey that selective sensing of UA in the presence of XA and vice-versa is possible at SPCE/GCN.

The amperometric method was employed to determine the wide linear range determination of UA at SPCE/GCN. Fig. 8 illustrates the amperometric i-t curve obtained for the oxidation of UA at SPCE/GCN. To a uniformly stirred 0.2 M PB solution, +0.40 V was applied constantly and UA concentration ranging from 0.2 µM to 1 mM was added at a constant interval of 50 s. The plot of concentration against the current was plotted and the response was linear against the selected concentration range with R2 = 0.9901 and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 24 nM (S/N=3). Table S2 compares the performance of the present SPCE/GCN sensor with the reported sensors with respect to linear range of concentration and LOD [36–42]. Dong et al. fabricated nitrogen doped carbon as electrochemical sensor for UA with a limited range of determination (0.5 to 300 µM) [40]. In the case of a hyperuricemic patient, the concentration of UA in blood serum is beyond 500 µM. But, the authors failed to demonstrate the determination of UA beyond 300 µM. Similarly, Lv et al. reported the sensing of UA by using a composite of porous GCN and multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). The authors determined UA from 0.2 to 20 µM alone where the selected range does not fall with the normal concentration of UA (400 µM) [37]. Murugan et al. reported the LOD of 4.45 µM using exfoliated GCN [36]. Among the reported papers listed in Table S2, the present modified electrode satisfactorily determines UA not only in a wide linear range concentration but also a reasonable LOD of 24.4 nM.

Further, the anti-interference ability of the SPCE/GCN was examined for the UA determination. Fig. S10 illustrates the i-t curve obtained for UA oxidation in the presence of possible interferences. Various metal cations, which often co-exist in blood serum were selected which include Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg+2 and Fe+2 and anions like Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, CH3COO− and C2O4 2− and biomolecules such as glucose, creatinine, urea and folic acid with a concentration of 1 mM were added to the homogeneously stirred solution of 0.2 M PB solution containing 1 µM UA. No change in the oxidation current of UA was noticed in the presence of the aforementioned interferences. This indicates that SPCE/GCN can be used to selectively determine UA containing 1000-fold higher concentration of above said possible interferents.

The stability of SPCE/GCN was examined by recording the DPVs for the oxidation of 1 mM UA in PB solution on day basis by keeping the electrode in open atmosphere at room temperature (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. S11, UA oxidation peak remains unchanged even after ten days, demonstrating the high stability of the SPCE/GCN. To analyze the reproducibility, GCN was electrodeposited on six different SPCEs and the oxidation of UA was recorded 10 repetitive measurements. The UA oxidation currents produced at six independent GCN/SPCEs show the RSD value of 1.1% (± 0.04%), revealing that it was exceptionally reproducible.

Fig. 9 displays the DPV of human blood serum sample in the absence and presence of UA and XA. In PB solution, no voltammetric response was observed for SPCE/GCN (curve a). But, on injecting blood serum to PB solution, two voltammetric peaks were obtained at 0.18 and 0.57 V (curve b). To the same solution, 20 µM commercial UA was added thrice successively (curve c, d and e). The current at 0.18 V was enhanced on each increment of 20 µM UA. This reveals that the anodic response obtained at 0.18 V was due to the presence of UA in blood serum. Similarly, the current obtained at 0.57 V was increased after the addition of 20 µM XA (curve f), This confirms the coexistence of XA along with UA in blood serum. The concentration of serum UA and the recoveries of UA were calculated and the corresponding results are given in Table 1 . Further, the quantification of UA by present method was validated with the enzymatic method, generally used in clinical laboratory. It can be seen from Table 1, the UA determined by DPV using SPCE/GCN closely matches with the data obtained from clinical method (Table 1). This indicates that SPCE/GCN could be used to quantify UA accurately in real samples.

The N-rich carbon nanomaterials, GCN, N-CDs and N-GQDs were synthesized and then electrodeposited on SPCE to test their sensing performance towards UA. The SPCE/GCN exhibited higher oxidation current with less positive potential for UA than SPCE/N-CDs and SPCE/N-GQDs. The cause for its better performance was ascribed to larger ESA and lower RCT in contrast to other two modified electrodes. The present modified electrode was also used to determine UA and XA simultaneously because the later coexist with the former in blood serum. The amperometric method was performed to determine UA in a wide linear range of 0.2 to 1000 µM and achieved the LOD of 24 nM (S/N=3). The same method was also used to quantify UA containing high concentration of possible interferences including different biomolecules. Finally, the application of the present sensor was accomplished by quantifying UA in human serum sample and the obtained results closely match with the enzymatic method.
Ajay Ajith: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. S. Abraham John: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial assistance from the Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi, (IDP/MED/04/2017) is indebtedly acknowledgd.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.140830.
